title: Tropical Cyclones
focus: Formation conditions and regional terminology of tropical cyclones.
rationale: The conditions for cyclone formation and the matching of regional names (e.g., Willy-willy, Typhoon) are classic, high-yield UPSC Prelims geography questions.
sectionExcerpt: Always form over oceans where sea surface temperature is greater than 26°C. Develop at latitudes usually greater than 5° from equator in the presence of Coriolis force. Terms for Tropical Cyclones: Hurricane – North Atlantic, Eastern North Pacific and South Pacific Ocean. Cyclone – Indian Ocean. Typhoon – Western North Pacific Ocean. Willy-willy: Eastern part of the Southern Indian Ocean.
evidenceBullets: Tropical cyclones require sea surface temperatures greater than 26°C to form.
They develop at latitudes usually greater than 5° from the equator due to the necessity of the Coriolis force.
Regional terms include Hurricane (North Atlantic/Pacific), Typhoon (Western North Pacific), and Willy-willy (Eastern part of the Southern Indian Ocean).

title: Miscellaneous Disaster and Weather Phenomena
focus: Definitions and characteristics of Earthquake Swarms, Swell Waves, Blowouts, and Mesoscale Convective Systems.
rationale: These distinct, short topics are highly testable as definitions or matching pairs in Prelims, particularly terms like 'Kallakkadal' and 'Earthquake Swarm'.
sectionExcerpt: Earthquake Swarm: Sequences of many earthquakes that occur in a relatively short period without a specific main shock. Swell Waves: Long-wavelength ocean waves travelling long distances away from their origin. Largely unaffected by local winds and often go undetected by satellites... Also called Kallakkadal waves in India. Blowout: Oil/gas well accident where underground pressure forces gas/oil to surface uncontrollably. Mesoscale Convective System (MCS): Collection of thunderstorms that act as a system. Can spread across entire state and last >12 hours.
evidenceBullets: Earthquake swarms are sequences of many earthquakes occurring in a short period without a specific main shock.
Swell waves, also known as Kallakkadal waves in India, are long-wavelength ocean waves largely unaffected by local winds.
A blowout is an uncontrolled release of gas or oil from a well due to underground pressure or valve failure.
A Mesoscale Convective System (MCS) is a collection of thunderstorms acting as a system that can last over 12 hours.

title: Indian National Centre for Ocean Information Services (INCOIS)
focus: Genesis, mandate, and key services provided by INCOIS.
rationale: INCOIS is a critical scientific organization under the Ministry of Earth Sciences. Its specific services, such as the Tsunami Early Warning System and Potential Fishing Zone Advisory, are standard UPSC targets.
sectionExcerpt: Established as an autonomous body in 1999 under Ministry of Earth Sciences (MoES). Unit of the Earth System Science Organization (ESSO). Mandate: Provide ocean information and advisory services to society, industry, government agencies and scientific community... Other INCOIS Services: Potential Fishing Zone (PFZ) Advisory, Tsunami Early Warning System, Ocean State Forecast (OSF), Coral Bleaching Alerts System etc.
evidenceBullets: INCOIS is an autonomous body established in 1999 under the Ministry of Earth Sciences (MoES).
It operates as a unit of the Earth System Science Organization (ESSO).
Key services include the Potential Fishing Zone (PFZ) Advisory, Tsunami Early Warning System, Ocean State Forecast, and Coral Bleaching Alerts System.

title: Disaster-Related Reports and Indices
focus: Key indices including the Global Drought Outlook, Disaster Risk Index (DRI), and District Flood Severity Index.
rationale: Reports and indices are high-yield for UPSC Prelims. The DRI's connection to the 15th Finance Commission and the specific components of the DFSI are strong factual anchors for questions.
sectionExcerpt: Global Drought Outlook, 2025 Released by: Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD). Disaster Risk Index (DRI)... Genesis: 15th Finance Commission (2021–26) formally adopted it to guide disaster funding. It integrated state’s DRI score into a formula for distributing NDRF and state contingency funds. Components: DRI uses probability of hazards (score of 70) and vulnerability (score of 30) to arrive at a composite score. District Flood Severity Index Developed by: Researchers from IIT Delhi and Gandhinagar... Consists of six factors classified in two groups: Variables representing occurrence of floods... Variables representing damage due to floods.
evidenceBullets: The Global Drought Outlook, 2025 is released by the OECD.
The Disaster Risk Index (DRI) was formally adopted by the 15th Finance Commission to guide disaster funding and distribute NDRF funds.
The DRI composite score is based on the probability of hazards (70) and vulnerability (30).
The District Flood Severity Index was developed by IIT Delhi and Gandhinagar researchers to quantify flood severity using occurrence and damage variables.
